title: Ezekiel 14
subtitle: Idolatrous Elders and the Three Righteous Men

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Ezekiel

sections.header: Verses 1-11 — Idols Set Up in Their Hearts

heb.paragraph: The elders come to consult Ezekiel, but God reveals that they have 'set up idols in their hearts.' This is Ezekiel's most profound contribution to the theology of idolatry: the problem is not merely external images but internal allegiance. Idolatry is first a condition of the heart before it is an act of worship. The heart-idol concept anticipates the new heart promise.

Their iniquity has become a stumbling block (mikhshol) — literally an obstacle that trips them. The phrase occurs seven times in Ezekiel and nowhere else, making it his distinctive vocabulary for the self-destructive nature of sin. Sin is not just offense against God but a trap the sinner sets for himself.

refs.note: The LORD looks at the heart — the principle underlying God's refusal to answer heart-idolaters.
You ask and do not receive because you ask with wrong motives — NT parallel to seeking God with an idolatrous heart.
Dear children, keep yourselves from idols — John's closing command echoes the heart-idol theology.

notes.note: Idols set up in their hearts — the location of idolatry shifts from the temple (ch. 8) to the human heart. External reform without internal transformation is worthless.
God will answer heart-idolaters according to their idols, not according to their question. The answer they receive will expose their true allegiance.
Any Israelite or foreigner who separates himself from God and takes idols into his heart — God himself will set his face against that person. Individual accountability.
If a prophet is enticed to give a false oracle, God has deceived that prophet as judgment. Both prophet and inquirer bear equal guilt.
Calvin sees this as the definitive statement on hypocrisy: outward consultation of God while maintaining inward loyalty to other gods. God exposes the disconnect between appearance and reality.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'set up idols in their hearts' (he'elu gilluleihem al libbam) literally means 'brought up their dung-idols upon their heart.' The crude language is deliberate: the heart is defiled by internal filth.

God 'deceiving' a prophet is a theological crux — compare 1 Kings 22:19-23 where God sends a lying spirit. The concept is that persistent pursuit of falsehood leads to God confirming the sinner in their delusion.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block reads this as the theological synthesis of chapters 8-13: temple abominations (ch. 8) + false prophecy (ch. 13) converge in heart-idolatry (ch. 14). The problem is not one institution but the human heart that corrupts every institution it touches. This diagnosis creates the need for the cardiac surgery of 11:19 and 36:26.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies this as a new form of prophetic torah — instruction on the conditions under which God will respond to inquiry. The passage functions as a rule for prophetic consultation: inner disposition determines whether the word is given or withheld.

hist.context: The elders of Israel come to sit before Ezekiel — ostensibly seeking God's word. But God refuses to answer them because they carry idols in their hearts. This creates a new category: heart-idolatry, distinct from temple-idolatry (ch. 8). You can sit before the prophet with no visible idol and still be an idolater. Internal loyalty, not external ritual, is the decisive factor.

sections.header: Verses 12-23 — Even Noah, Daniel, and Job Could Not Save

heb.paragraph: Three legendary righteous men: Noah who survived the flood, Daniel who survived the lion's den (or possibly the ancient Dan'el of Ugaritic literature), and Job who endured unjust suffering. Even these paragons of righteousness could save only themselves — not even their own children. The principle of individual accountability overrides even legendary merit.

refs.note: Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD — but even his righteousness could only save his own household.
Job was blameless and upright — the paradigm of righteous suffering, now invoked as insufficient to avert national judgment.
Even if Moses and Samuel stood before me, my heart would not turn to this people — Jeremiah uses the same rhetorical device with different exemplars.
Each of us will give an account of himself to God — the NT restates Ezekiel's principle of individual accountability.

notes.note: Noah, Daniel, and Job represent three types of righteousness: Noah preserved through global judgment, Daniel maintained integrity in a pagan court, Job endured innocent suffering. All three are insufficient to cover national sin.
Not even their own sons and daughters could be saved by their righteousness. Vicarious merit does not transfer across generations.
How much worse when God sends all four judgments at once — sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague together.
Yet survivors will emerge — and when the exiles see their conduct, they will understand why God destroyed Jerusalem. The survivors serve as proof of justified judgment.

Calvin uses this passage to teach the limits of intercessory merit: no saint's righteousness can cover another's sin. Each person stands before God individually. This undermines any theology of transferred merit from saints to sinners.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The spelling 'Daniel' here (dan'el, without the yod) differs from the book of Daniel (daniyye'l) and matches the Ugaritic hero Dan'el from the Aqhat epic. This has led many scholars to identify him as the ancient legendary figure rather than Ezekiel's contemporary.
The four judgments (sword, famine, wild beasts, plague) correspond to the covenant curses of Leviticus 26:22-26.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block argues for the biblical Daniel: despite the spelling variant, the pairing of three men known for their righteousness before God fits the canonical Daniel better than the Ugaritic figure. The three represent different eras and contexts of faithfulness — pre-flood, exilic, and patriarchal — creating a comprehensive claim that no era's righteousness can avert the current judgment.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli favors the Ugaritic Dan'el identification, arguing that the trio represents ancient legendary figures of exemplary righteousness known throughout the Near East. Ezekiel draws on international wisdom tradition to make a universal point: even the most renowned righteous men in human memory cannot save a nation under divine judgment.

hist.context: The oracle employs a four-fold structure: sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague — the four judgments of covenant curse (Lev 26). For each judgment, the same verdict: even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were in the land, they could deliver only themselves. The identity of Daniel is debated: some scholars see the biblical Daniel (Ezekiel's contemporary in Babylon), while others identify the ancient Dan'el known from Ugaritic texts — a legendary wise man paired with other ancient figures.

rows.label: vv.6-11
rows.text: Call to Repentance and Warning to Prophets

rows.label: vv.12-20
rows.text: Noah, Daniel, and Job: Individual Righteousness

rows.label: vv.21-23
rows.text: Four Judgments and the Surviving Remnant

lit.note: Two connected oracles: heart-idolatry that blocks access to God (vv.1-11) and the limits of vicarious righteousness (vv.12-23). Individual accountability is the unifying theme.

themes.note: Justice at 9: individual accountability is the central theme. Judgment at 9: even legendary righteousness cannot avert national doom. Worship at 7: heart-idolatry redefines the nature of false worship.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Idolatrous Elders and the Three Righteous Men

translations.text: “ ‘When any of the Israelites or any foreigner residing in Israel separate themselves from me and set up idols in their hearts and put a wicked stumbling block before their faces and then go to a prophet to inquire of me, I the Lord will answer them myself.

For any one of the house of Israel, or of the strangers who sojourn in Israel, who separates himself from me, taking his idols into his heart and putting the stumbling block of his iniquity before his face, and yet comes to a prophet to consult me through him, I the LORD will answer him myself.
“Son of man, these men have set up idols in their hearts and put wicked stumbling blocks before their faces. Should I let them inquire of me at all?
“Son of man, these men have taken their idols into their hearts, and set the stumbling block of their iniquity before their faces. Should I indeed let myself be consulted by them?

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Idolatrous Elders and the Three Righteous Men within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Even if Moses and Samuel stood before me, my heart would not turn to this people — Jeremiah uses the same rhetorical device with different exemplars.

coaching.tip: The elders come to consult Ezekiel, but God sees 'idols set up in their hearts' (v. 3). The problem isn't external — they're not carrying statues — it's internal: their loyalties are divided before they open their mouths. God refuses to be consulted by hearts that have already decided. The chapter defines the prerequisite for hearing God: not sinlessness but sincerity. Divided loyalty gets no answer.

'Even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were in it, they could save only themselves' (v. 14). Three legendary righteous men — representing different eras and even different nationalities (Job isn't Israelite) — cannot intercede for a corrupt society. Individual righteousness protects the individual, not the nation. Ezekiel dismantles the assumption that a few good people can cover for systemic sin. Corporate judgment requires corporate repentance.
